description:
You are an infrastructure engineer automating the provisioning of a fleet of Nginx-based
    containers.  
    A vendor-supplied, pre-built Nginx configuration file is already present on disk and a
    reference checksum file shipped by the vendor is also in place.

    Paths already present on the system:
      • /home/user/configs/nginx.conf                – the configuration file you must verify  
      • /home/user/checksums.txt                     – the vendor’s reference SHA-256 checksum  
      • /home/user/logs/                             – directory reserved for your logs (exists, writable)

    Goal  
    1. Perform SHA-256 checksum verification of /home/user/configs/nginx.conf against the
       expected value stored in /home/user/checksums.txt.  
    2. Save **only** the raw, single-line result that the verification command prints
       (no extra headers, timestamps, or explanatory text) to the file  
       /home/user/logs/nginx_checksum.log.

       The automated test will look for exactly one line in that log file, in the canonical
       format produced by the standard utility

           <absolute-path-to-file>: OK

       or

           <absolute-path-to-file>: FAILED

       therefore the line must look precisely like one of the following two examples
       (including the colon and the single space before OK/FAILED):

           /home/user/configs/nginx.conf: OK
           /home/user/configs/nginx.conf: FAILED

    Constraints  
    • Achieve the entire task with a single terminal command (shell redirection is allowed).  
    • Do not modify /home/user/configs/nginx.conf or /home/user/checksums.txt.  
    • Do not add any extra text to /home/user/logs/nginx_checksum.log; it must contain
      the single verification line and nothing else.

    Success criteria  
    • /home/user/logs/nginx_checksum.log exists.  
    • The file contains exactly one line and that line is in one of the two
      allowed formats shown above.  
    • The status in that line correctly reflects whether the checksum matched the
      vendor reference value.
truth:
Initial state (before the agent starts):

    1. /home/user/configs/nginx.conf  
       – File exists and is empty (zero bytes).

    2. SHA-256 of an empty file (used below):  
       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

    3. /home/user/checksums.txt  
       – Contains one line, exactly (two spaces between hash and path):

       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  /home/user/configs/nginx.conf

    4. /home/user/logs/  
       – Directory exists and is writable by the normal user.  
       – File /home/user/logs/nginx_checksum.log does NOT yet exist.

    Verification logic used by the automated grader:

       a) The grader will execute  
          sha256sum -c /home/user/checksums.txt  
          to decide whether the result should be “OK” or “FAILED”.

       b) It will then read /home/user/logs/nginx_checksum.log and check that:

          • Exactly one line is present.  
          • The line is either  
              "/home/user/configs/nginx.conf: OK"  (newline)  
            or  
              "/home/user/configs/nginx.conf: FAILED"  (newline)  
          • The suffix (“OK” or “FAILED”) matches the outcome obtained in step a.

    Note: given the provided files, the correct verification result is **OK**.
